coredata.dc:title: Design 3D hierarchical flower-shaped NH4V4O10/N-GQDs/cellulose nanocomposites as electrode materials for supercapacitor application
coredata.dc:creator: Ghiyasiyan-Arani, Maryam
Ansarinejad, Hanieh
Shabani-Nooshabadi, Mehdi
Dawi, Elmuez A.
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, materials based on carbon dioxide compounds and transition metal oxide nanocomposites with desirable synergistic properties have attracted a great deal of attention as potential candidates for high efficiency electrochemical supercapacitors. Our work presents the development of high-performance supercapacitor electrodes using the 3D-flower-shaped NH4V4O10 microstructures combined with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and cellulose (NHV-NGC). A simple in-situ hydrothermal synthesis was used to synthesize the NHV-NGC. Various methods are employed to characterize the physiochemical properties of the synthesized composite. In addition, cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to examine the electrochemical behavior of the materials as designed. According to the GCD profiles, the optimized NHV-NGC electrode has a specific capacitance of 1852.9 Fg−1 (878 Cg−1) at a current density of 2 Ag−1 in 3.0 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. As well, the specific capacitance of the NHV-NGC electrode is about three times greater than the specific capacitance of the NHV electrode (631 Fg−1 (300 Cg−1). The electrode shows a high retention specific capacitance of 83.5% after 3000 charge-discharge cycles at a scanning rate of 100 mVs−1. Additionally, the fabricated supercapacitor exhibits an energy density of 57.8 Whkg−1 at a power density of 473.9 Wkg−1. Based on the GCD plots of the optimized NHV-NGC electrode, the specific capacitance was calculated to be 1852.9, 1224.9, 981.8, and 286.2 Fg−1 (878, 587.1, 466, and 137.1 Cg−1) at 2, 3, 4, and 5 Ag−1, respectively. Synergistic effects between the three-dimensional flower-shaped NH4V4O10 microstructures, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and cellulose in the nanocomposites structure resulted in extraordinary conductivity, excellent electrochemical performance, and the development of suitable routes for rapid ion/electron transport via reversible redox reactions. In terms of electrochemical performance, the NHV-NGC nanocomposite is proving to be a promising candidate for energy storage devices such as supercapacitors.

head.ce:title: Design 3D hierarchical flower-shaped NH4V4O10/N-GQDs/cellulose nanocomposites as electrode materials for supercapacitor application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, materials based on carbon dioxide compounds and transition metal oxide nanocomposites with desirable synergistic properties have attracted a great deal of attention as potential candidates for high efficiency electrochemical supercapacitors. Our work presents the development of high-performance supercapacitor electrodes using the 3D-flower-shaped NH4V4O10 microstructures combined with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and cellulose (NHV-NGC). A simple in-situ hydrothermal synthesis was used to synthesize the NHV-NGC. Various methods are employed to characterize the physiochemical properties of the synthesized composite. In addition, cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to examine the electrochemical behavior of the materials as designed. According to the GCD profiles, the optimized NHV-NGC electrode has a specific capacitance of 1852.9 Fg−1 (878 Cg−1) at a current density of 2 Ag−1 in 3.0 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. As well, the specific capacitance of the NHV-NGC electrode is about three times greater than the specific capacitance of the NHV electrode (631 Fg−1 (300 Cg−1). The electrode shows a high retention specific capacitance of 83.5% after 3000 charge-discharge cycles at a scanning rate of 100 mVs−1. Additionally, the fabricated supercapacitor exhibits an energy density of 57.8 Whkg−1 at a power density of 473.9 Wkg−1. Based on the GCD plots of the optimized NHV-NGC electrode, the specific capacitance was calculated to be 1852.9, 1224.9, 981.8, and 286.2 Fg−1 (878, 587.1, 466, and 137.1 Cg−1) at 2, 3, 4, and 5 Ag−1, respectively. Synergistic effects between the three-dimensional flower-shaped NH4V4O10 microstructures, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and cellulose in the nanocomposites structure resulted in extraordinary conductivity, excellent electrochemical performance, and the development of suitable routes for rapid ion/electron transport via reversible redox reactions. In terms of electrochemical performance, the NHV-NGC nanocomposite is proving to be a promising candidate for energy storage devices such as supercapacitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent years have seen an increase in global energy issues, which have led to humans being confronted with a serious crisis that cannot be neglected [1]. The global energy challenge can be reduced by utilizing renewable and clean energy sources (solar, wind, and tide) and developing efficient energy storage technologies (fuel cells, rechargeable batteries, and supercapacitors) [2–4]. As an alternative energy storage system, supercapacitors or ultracapacitors, also called electrochemical capacitors, have been attracted to the attention of industry and academia societies due to their desirable characteristics such as eco-friendly, high power density, superior charging/discharging rates, exceptional cycle stability, and low price [4–7]. Based on their energy storage process, supercapacitors are generally classified into three groups: (i) electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLCs), which stored energy by electrostatic force; (ii) pseudo-capacitors (PCs), which accumulate energy through a redox reaction; (iii) hybrid supercapacitors, which collect energy through a redox reaction and electrostatically [8,9]. Despite the long life cycle of EDLCs with carbon-based materials (CNTs, graphene oxide, activated carbon, cellulose, etc.), they suffer from low conductivity and poor cyclability. Furthermore, PCs with different active materials such as metal oxides, metal sulfides, and conductive polymers have high specific capacitance and great power/energy density, but their lifetime is limited. Hybrid capacitors are capable of overcoming these defects by simultaneously combining the advantages of both EDLCs and PCs [10,11]. Due to the critical role played by active electrode materials in supercapacitor performance, recent research has focused on active materials with superior supercapacitor performance. Among those materials, layered materials (LMs) with unique physicochemical properties have proven themselves to be capable materials for electrochemical energy storage. Materials used in LMs include inorganic silicates, phosphates, titanates, layered hydroxides, graphite, metal sulfides, vanadium pentoxide, and molybdenum trioxide. It is well known that LMs exhibit strong anisotropic bonding, in which two-dimensional sheets are bonded internally to adjacent layers [12,13]. Among the LMs, the vanadium-based materials are used as beneficial active electrode materials for energy storage technologies such as supercapacitors and Li-ion batteries. With introduced various cations such as Na+, Li+, K+, Cu2+, and Mn2+ into their layered structures, the electrochemical efficiency and cycle performance of the layered vanadium-based materials have been enhanced [3]. Comparing vanadium-based compounds with polyanionic compounds and other vanadium-based compounds, vanadium-based compounds have several notable advantages. In part, this can be attributed to the stratified nature of their composition and their large theoretical capacity [14,15]. Weina Deng et al. synthesized the chemical (NH4)2Co2V10O28·16 H2O/ (NH4)2V10O25·8 H2O by a hydrothermal method. High-performance aqueous Zn-ion batteries used this chemical as the cathode. After enduring 1000 cycles, the electrode maintains a discharge capacity of 201mAh g−1 and has a capacity retention rate of 82.1% resulting from 390.1mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 [16].

There has been considerable interest in NH4V4O10 (Ammonium vanadate bronze) with large spacing between interlayers and different oxidation states in recent decades [17]. Due to the many oxidation states of vanadium (V) and the variety of coordination polyhedra, vanadium layered oxides have gained much attention among electrode materials. In layered ammonium vanadium oxides, the layer spacing is greater than in V2O5, making it possible to insert and remove guest ions without significantly harming the host material [18]. In comparison to V2O5, vanadium bronze materials (MxV4O10) exhibit greater ionic and electronic conductivities, making them potentially ideal electrode materials. These substances are layered, with intercalated cations within the VO6 layers that are shared at the edges and corners. Due to the fact that the presence of NH4+ ions in the bilayer increases the interlayer gap and facilitates the addition and removal of ions, NH4V4O10 with double layers has recently attracted particular attention. [19]. Vanadium oxide is stacked, and ammonium ions are implanted within those layers to form ammonium vanadate. The N-H.O link between the ammonium ion and the vanadium oxide layer is more stable in the layered structure of ammonium vanadate than it is in vanadium oxide [20].

In contrast, NH4V4O10 structures exhibit good specific capacitance, but suffer from poor cycle stability. It is therefore possible to overcome the defects of ammonium vanadate-based materials by using Carbonous materials such as graphene quantum dots and cellulose as conductive additives with a high specific surface area. A comparison of vanadium-based materials used as electrode materials in supercapacitors is presented in Table 1. NH4V4O10 has been studied in terms of synthesis details in previous research. A study by Wang and coworkers describes the synthesis of NH4V4O10 micro flowers with a radius of about 5 m over a period of 20 h for the purpose of nickel ion batteries [21]. An NH4V4O10 photocatalyst was prepared over a long period of time (24 h) exhibiting nanobelt morphology with measurements of 550–600 nm in length, 150 nm in width, and 50 nm in thickness [22]. A zinc ion battery using Ti-doped NH4V4O10 was manufactured by hydrothermal method at 180 ⁰ C for 24 h, exhibiting a morphology of agglomerated micro-belts with a width of 800 nm and a length of 3 µm [23]. Stacked nanosheet (∼ 1 µm in width) of NH4V4O10 was prepared with source of V2O5 which is hardly soluble. Furthermore, the solvent used in this procedure is ethylene glycol, which is not environmentally friendly [24]. Other research has reported the hydrothermal synthesis of NH4V4O10 nanoribbons at 473 K for 12 h, which is at high temperatures and long times [25]. 2D nanobelts structure with a width of 50–200 nm was synthesized by S. Le et.al at 453 K for 24 h, which shows high thickness of NH4V4O10 structures in high temperature and longtime hydrothermal synthesis [26].

During the last decade, cellulose has gained increasing attention as an active material in high-performance energy storage systems due to its unique properties such as high porosity, hydroxyl groups on the surfaces, abundance and renewable raw material, low cost, non-toxic, and suitability for industrial manufacturing. Moreover, cellulose shows high power density, excellent reversibility and a long cycle life, making it an excellent choice for use in high-power density supercapacitors [27]. A variety of raw materials and extraction methods have been reported for the production of cellulose materials, including catalyzed hydrolysis, enzymatic hydrolysis, ultrasound acid hydrolysis, and HCl hydrolysis using a source of cotton fabric, waste fabric, pulp fiber, etc. [28–31].

Quantum dots based on graphene are an intriguing class of quantum dots exhibiting exceptional properties in single or multilayered structures with nanoscale sizes categorized in graphene-based families. In addition, doping carbon-based materials (activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, and graphene quantum dotes) with heteroatoms, such as nitrogen, boron, and sulfur, can enhance electrochemical performance of pure carbon-based materials for supercapacitor applications [27]. Yuwan Han et.al reported a new supercapacitor GOQDs @NiAl-LDH electrode material. The electrochemical results show that schemed material have high specific capacitance (to 869 F g−1 at the current density of 1 A g−1.), and excellent cycle stability (69.6% after 2000 cycles) [32]. Qing Chen et. al. reported the design of Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) onto the three-dimensional graphene (3DG) by a electrochemical deposition procedure which revelations a high specific capacitance of 268 F g−1 [33].

In this work, we have attempted to enhance electrochemical performance of 3D-flower-shaped NH4V4O10 microstructures by combining nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with cellulose, in accordance with the mentioned information about the synthesis and properties of NH4V4O10, cellulose and GQDs. An ecofriendly hydrothermal process of NH4V4O10/N-GQDs/Cellulose (NHV-NGC) was used to synthesize NHV-NGC nanocomposites in a short time and at low temperatures to achieve this purpose. The performance of the supercapacitor was also examined in relation to the amount of in situ synthesized NGC added to the NH4V4O10. The principal objectives of this work are as follows:
(a)
Facile synthesis of NH4V4O10/N-GQDs/Cellulose (NHV-NGC) nanocomposites using low-cost precursors.
(b)
In-situ hydrolysis process for the synthesis of N-GQDs/Cellulose compounds synchronously.
(c)
Ternary nanocomposite (NH4V4O10/N-GQDs/Cellulose) acts as an efficient electrode for supercapacitors applications.
(d)
The presence of GQDs and cellulose improved the electrochemical activity and stability of flower-shaped NH4V4O10 structures.
(e)
Effect of N-GQDs/Cellulose(NGC) percentage on the supercapacitor behaviors of NH4V4O10 microflowers.

The initial components were obtained from Merck and utilized without additional purification consisting NH4VO3 (≥ 99.0%), NH4NO3 (≥ 95.0%), oxalic acid (99.5%), citric acid (99.5%), ethylene diamine (≥ 99.0%) and Na2CO3 (≥ 99.9%).

Nicolet Magna-550 spectrometer with KBr pellets, Philips-Xpert Pro X-ray diffractometer with Ni-filtered Cu Ka radiation, and LEO-1455VP FE-SEM were used to characterize the samples. The samples were also characterized using a 200 kV Philips EM208 transmission electron microscope and a 20 kV hasten voltage for EDS, BET, and BJH analyses (Tristar 3000, Micromeritics). CV and GCD techniques were conducted through ELECTROANALYZER SYSTEM µIII auto 71174. The impedance spectra were also measured across a frequency range of 0.1 to 100 kHz. NOVA (v2.1) software (Metrohm/Autolab) was used to analyze the results. SAMA 500, electroanalyzer system (Iran) tested the potentiostat/galvanostat for cycle life stability.

Cotton was readily available for purchase as a raw material for making cellulose. Alkali processes were used to disinfect waste cotton by eliminating non-cellulosic chemicals. Cotton was treated with aqueous Na2CO3 solution for 30 min at 60 ⁰ [34]. A water bath was used to treat cotton while continuously stirring dissolved Na2CO3 (2 g in 200 ml DI water). A series of rinses in DI water were then conducted until the pH level of the cotton reached 7. Afterwards, the cotton was dried in an oven for 24 h.

As part of the in-situ synthesis of Nitrogen-doped Graphene quantum dots/Cellulose (NGC) composites, the hydrothermal method was applied using ethylene diamine and citric acid as precursors. Under a stirrer, 0.25 g of citric acid (0.0013 mol) was dissolved in 30 ml of DI water and the ethylene diamine (0.08 ml-0.0011 mol) was mixed with it. A piece of cotton (3 ×3 cm2) is immersed in the provided solution and then placed in a sealed autoclave for ten hours at a temperature of 200 °C. As a result of the reaction, the resultant NGC is removed from the solution and washed several times with DI water.

The first step was to prepare solution "A" by combining 0.5 g of NH4VO3 (1.8 mol), 1.077 g of oxalic acid (2.8 mol), and 0.181 g of NH4NO3 (1 mol) in DI water. In the following steps, the obtained NGC (in Section 2.3) was dispersed in the resultant solution "A" and the 50 ml beaker was transferred to the sealed autoclave for four hours at 180 °C. NH4V4O10/N-GQDs/Cellulose composites marked as NHV-NGC were finally obtained after being washed with DI water and dried in an oven. In order to prepare nanocomposites, different weight ratios of NHV:NGC were used, such as 30%:70%, 50%:50%, and 70%:30%, which are denoted by NHV-NGC1, NHV-NGC2, and NHV-NGC3, respectively.

To synthesize the pristine hierarchical flower-shaped NH4V4O10 microstructures (NHV), the same hydrothermal conditions applied to NHV-NGC were used without NGC. A solution of 0.5 g NH4VO3 was dissolved in DI water at 70 °C under constant stirring. As a next step, 1.077 g oxalic acid and 0.181 g NH4NO3 were dissolved in DI water and mixed with NH4VO3 solution to prepare an orange solution. After obtaining the solution, it was transferred into a sealed 50 ml autoclave at 180 degrees Celsius for four hours. Following the precipitation, ethanol and DI water were used to wash and dry the green precipitate ( Schematic 1).

It is first necessary to prepare the working electrodes for the electrochemical investigation of the electrodes as-prepared. In order to achieve this, the slurry containing 85 wt% of each microstructure (NH4V4O10, NHV-NGC1, NHV-NGC2, and NHV-NGC3) was mixed with 5 wt% of PVDF and 10 wt% of acetylene black in N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP). We spread the homogenized slurry on a strip of nickel foam (NF) (1 ×2 cm2) with a thickness of 1.5–2.5 mm (=0.15–0.45 g/cm3) and dried it at 60 °C for 24 h to eliminate the solvent. In terms of the mass loading of the electrodes as-prepared (NHV/NF, NHV-NGC1/NF, NHV-NGC2/NF, and NHV-NGC3/NF), the range was from 2 to 4 mg cm−2 for all fully-dried electrodes. As part of an electrochemical test using a three-electrode setup, the prepared electrode served as the working electrode, a platinum plate served as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl (3.0 M KCl) served as the reference electrode. In an alkaline electrolyte with a concentration of 3.0 M KOH, the tests were performed using the electro-analyzer system m-III auto 71174. A galvanostat-potentiostat with gps software is being used.

In order to determine the specific capacitance (Cs) of the NHV/NF, NHV-NGC1/NF, NHV-NGC2/NF, and NHV-NGC3/NF electrodes, the following equation was used: [35]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystalline phase, chemical structure and elemental analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1(a-c) shows XRD diffractograms for synthesized samples of NGC, NHV and NHV-NGC. XRD diffractogram shown in Fig. 1a shows several peaks at 14.62, 16.65, 22.87, and 34.42⁰ that correspond to cellulose (1−10), (110), and (200) planes (JCPDS = 00–050-2241) [28]. The formation of GQDs can be confirmed due to the presence of a peak at 34.22, which is related to the plane of (123) of carbon (JCPDS = 00–050-0926). Fig. 1b displays the XRD diffractogram for synthesized NH4V4O10, which is consistent with the reference pattern with the JCPDS code 00–026-0096. The located peaks at 2Theta of 25.54, 27.44, 31.12, 34.29, 36.70, 46.85, 47.62, 49.77, 50.53, 55.48, 57.89, 59.80, 61.07 and 70.84 degree that were assigned to (110), (111), (400), (401), (−312), (−205), (005), (020), (021), (−514), (0060, (4201), 9603) and (−225) planes of the monoclinic NH4V4O10. The designed nanocomposite based on NHV and NGC represents all related peaks of NH4V4O10, N-GQDs and cellulose in Fig. 1c which confirms the synthesis of NHV-NGC nanocomposites without any impurities.

Fig. 2(a-c) illustrates the FT-IR spectra of NGC, NHV, and NHV-NGC samples at wavelengths between 400 and 4000 cm−1. FT-IR spectra of NGC indicate that the peaks at 3346 and 2901 cm−1 correspond to C–H stretching vibrations and O–H stretching vibrations, respectively. It was found that the C-O stretching vibration, the C-O of the aromatic ring, and the C-O-C pyranose ring skeleton, respectively, corresponded to peaks located at 1651, 1367, and 1162 cm−1. A symmetric CH2 bending vibration is associated with the absorption band at 1433 cm−1. The situated peak at 1056 cm−1 was attributed to the C-O-C pyranose ring. A stretching band of C-O is also visible at 1112 cm−1 [39]. Located at 1557 cm−1, the peak represents the vibration and deformation bands of N − H. At 667 cm−1, the vibration of =C-H is visible [40]. Based on Fig. 2b, the FT-IR spectrum of NHV shows several peaks at 528, 807, 991, 1404, 3177 cm−1 related to the characteristic peaks of NH4V4O10. In a nutshell, the signal at 807 cm−1 characterized the asymmetric stretching of the V-O-V bond, whereas the peak at 528 cm−1 defined the symmetric stretching. VO stretching bond appeared at 991 cm−1. Furthermore, the band at 3177 and 1404 cm−1 were ascribed the asymmetric stretching and bending vibration corresponding to N-H mode of NH4 + group [17]. Finally, the FT-IR spectrum of NH4V4O10/N-GQDs-Cellulose nanocomposite shows both characteristic peaks of NGC and NHV in Fig. 2c.

An elemental analysis was conducted on the NGC, the NHV and the NHV-NGC nanocomposite, which is shown in Fig. 3(a-c). Based on the EDS result for NGC (Fig. 3a), nitrogen, carbon, and oxygen can be identified. As shown in Fig. 3b, nitrogen, vanadium, and oxygen are present in the NHV sample. As a result, the nanocomposite of NHV-NGC shows all elements that are present in N-GQDs, such as cellulose and NH4V4O10 (Fig. 3c).

Electron microscopy was used to examine the structure of the synthetic materials. The FE-SEM images of the NGC, NHV, and NHV-NGC are shown in Fig. 4(a-c), (d-f), and (g-i). It is evident from Fig. 4(a-c) that the fiber substrate is covered by GQD structures in the cellulose-based texture with the decoration of N-GQDs. In Fig. 4(d-f), the morphology of NH4V4O10 exhibits flower-shaped microstructures that assemble with nanoribbons to form three-dimensional hierarchical structures. Nanoribbons have a thickness of approximately 10 nm and flower spheres have a diameter of approximately 5 m. As shown in Fig. 4(g-i), FE-SEM images of NHV-NGC composites confirm the presence of cellulose fibers and ribbon NH4V4O10 structures.

A possible mechanism for the growth and formation of 3D hierarchical flower-shaped structures can be summarized as follows: It has been demonstrated that growth kinetics can be controlled in a manner that brings about shape-controlled crystal growth. As there is no template present during the reaction, the starting ingredients, which include ammonium vanadate, oxalic acid, and ammonium nitrate, may play an important role. Accordingly, explaining the process using the concept of directed attachment crystal formation may be more persuasive. In the initial step, NH4V4O10 nanocrystals were generated using the hydrothermal method. Synthesis involves arranging crystals into 2D nanoribbons and assembling these ribbons into flower-shaped structures. Due to this, 3D hierarchical NH4V4O10 microstructures are formed from well-defined 2D NH4V4O10 nanoribbon structures [41]. As a result of the high surface energy, newly produced nuclei are thermodynamically unstable. It is because they have just been generated. It follows that the nuclei have a tendency to cluster together in order to reduce the amount of energy lost at the interface; consequently, the supersaturated nuclei may gather together. The concentration of both the precursors and the reactants decreases as the reaction proceeds. Furthermore, as the reaction proceeds, certain active sites on the surface of the first precursor aggregations may begin to expand in a direction that is congruent with the direction in which the reaction environment continues to supply reactants. Fresh particles were deposited on the little elevated sections, which caused them to agglomerate along certain directions, resulting in the formation of tiny ribbons. It is possible for these ribbons to grow in particular directions. Consequently, 3D hierarchical superstructures are organized into a well-structured flower-shaped form [42].

TEM and HR-TEM images in different magnifications were provided for identification of NHV-NGC composites with detailed information. TEM images can deliver structural details as well as crystallinity data of samples. Fig. 5 shows the presence of amorphous parts which related to Cellulose and GQDs with nanoribbons unites of NHV. According to Fig. 5d, the HR-TEM image specifies the facets of crystalline phase with the space fringes of 0.259 nm are corresponded to d(401) of NH4V4O10.

Fig. 6 presents the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms as well as the BJH pore size distribution plot of pure NHV and NHV-NGC nanocomposites. Based on the IUPAC classification, obtained isotherms interrelated to the IV-type hysteresis loop [43]. This isotherm is commonly exhibited by many mesoporous industrial adsorbents and inorganic oxide xerogels, of which the monolayered surface coverage of the pore walls is followed by capillary condensation or pore filling. The adsorption ceases, once all the pores are completely filled. The adsorption behavior in mesopores is determined by the adsorbent adsorptive interactions and also by the interactions between the molecules in the condensed state. In this case, the initial monolayer-multilayer adsorption on the mesopore walls, which takes the same path as the corresponding part of a Type II isotherm, is followed by pore condensation [44,45].

A BET surface area of the NHV was approximately 19 m2g−1, and the addition of NGC increased the BET surface area to approximately 36 m2g−1. In both pristine NHV and NHV-NGC nanocomposites, there was a narrow distribution of pore diameters of approximately 2 nm. For NHV-NGC, the total pore volume was intensified by 0.055 cm3g−1, as compared to 0.030 cm3g−1 for pristine NHV. In comparison with pristine NHV and NHV-NGC composites, the mean pore radius is 6.30 and 6.10 nm, respectively. As a consequence of the introduction of N-GQDs/Cellulose substrate and the larger interlayer spacing of fabricated NHV-NGC toward pristine NHV, more electrolyte ions were able to be stored, resulting in NHV-NGC having a higher electrochemical activity than pristine NHV [46].

The electrochemical efficiency of the as-synthesized electrodes (i.e. NHV/NF and NHV-NGC/NF) were studied using CV and GCD analysis in three electrode electrochemical systems contain Ag/AgCl (3.0 M KCl) as reference electrode, Pt plate as counter electrode and as-fabricated electrodes as working electrode, respectively. CV plots of NHV-NGC with different amount of N-GQDs/Cellulose (based on Table 2) at a scan rate of 50 mV s− 1 at the potential window from 0.1 to 0.57 V were shown in the Fig. 7a. According to the CV curves, the strong redox peaks indicate that the charge storage mechanism was the surface redox reaction which is different from EDLC. Also, the results show that the NHV-NGC2/NF electrode has the largest CV enclosed area which demonstrated the excellent capacitive behavior of as-designed electrodes. According to the Eq. 1, the specific capacitance of the NHV-NGC2/NF is 1.75 times more than the specific capacitance of the NHV/NF which suggests the (i) synergistic effects between the components of the 3D hierarchical flower-shaped NH4V4O10/N-GQDs/Cellulose nanocomposites [47], (ii) high surface area and porosity of NHV-NGC2 due to the presence of cellulose based texture [48], (iii) high electrical conductivity, which enables an improvement in the electrochemical performance of the material owing to the presence of N-GQDs [49], and (iv) the desirable morphology of NHV-NGC2 which can facilitate diffusion contact between electrolyte ions during CV analysis [50]. Furthermore, by increasing the amount of N-GQDs/Cellulose in finally nanocomposites, the available active sites decrease and the NHV-NGC3/NF electrode exhibits lower specific capacitance. Fig. 7b, show the specific capacitance in (Fg−1 and Cg−1) of NHV/NF (1272.5 Fg−1 (636.3 Cg−1)), NHV-NGC1/NF (1454.8 Fg−1 (727.4 Cg−1)), NHV-NGC2/NF (1760.2 Fg−1 (880.1 Cg−1)) and NHV-NGC3/NF (510.1 Fg−1 (255 Cg−1)). Fig. 7c represents the GCD curves of NHV/NF, NHV-NGC1/NF, NHV-NGC2/NF and NHV-NGC3/NF electrodes tested at a current density of 2Ag−1 a potential window between 0.1 V and 0. 57 V. The results from GCD plots, like the CV curves demonstrated NHV-NGC2 as the optimum sample. All the GCD plots show deviance from the ideal triangular state due to the storing charge through the pseudo-capacitance process. The discharge time of NHV, NHV-NGC1/NF, NHV-NGC2/NF and NHV-NGC3/NF is 150 s, 225.2 s, 439 s and 40.9 s respectively. Based on the discharge times and Eq. 2, the specific capacitance of NHV-NGC2 significantly greater in comparison to the other electrodes. The results show the optimum amount of N-GQDs/Cellulose improved electrochemical behavior of NHV-NGC2/NF. Fig. 7d exhibits the specific capacitance for all as-prepared samples at 2 A g−1 discharge current densities (based on Eq. 2). Based on the results, the specific capacitance of NHV, NHV-NGC1/NF, NHV-NGC2/NF and NHV-NGC3/NF at 2Ag−1 is found to be 631.3 Fg−1 (300 Cg−1), 955.3 Fg−1 (450.4 Cg−1), 1852.9 Fg−1 (878 Cg−1) and 173.5 Fg−1 (81.8 Cg−1), respectively. The remarkably enhancement in specific capacitance value of nanocomposite is caused by the synergistic effect of NH4V4O10, N-GQDs and Cellulose of the ternary composite. Additionally, the layered morphology of cellulose improves ionic and electronic transport through the electrode/electrolyte interface cause to create a high specific capacitance [51]. Fig. 8a exhibits the CV curves of NHV-NGC2 in aqueous electrolyte of 3.0 M KOH with a series of scan-rates. Based on the results, the oxidation processes with the oxidation potential of ∼0.45 V (Ep,a∼0.45 V) and reduction processes with the reduction potentials of ∼0.3 V and ∼0.25 V (Ep,a∼0.35 V, 0.25 V) were performed. Also, the integrating the area under the CV plots and cathodic /anodic currents were enhanced by increasing the scan rates. However, the shape of CV voltammograms almost unchanged, which indicative desirable electrochemical reversibility of electrodes [52]. Furthermore, by increasing the scan rate, the Ep,a and Ep,c shifted symmetrically to positive and negative directions, respectively. Furthermore, according to CV profiles, the CV enclosed area of NHV-NGC2 showed a decreasing trend with increasing scan rates, this phenomenon is happening because of the fast redox reaction and the decrease of electrolyte ion diffusion into the active material (Fig. 8b) [53]. For the purpose of estimating the specific capacitance of the NHV-NGC2/NF electrode, GCD technique was carried out at various current densities from 2 to 5 Ag−1 in a potential ranging between 0.1 V and 0.57 V. Fig. 8b shows that in accordance with the results, the mechanism of charge storage for NHV-NGC2/NF is pseudo-capacitance, and the noticeable faradaic redox reactions throughout the charging and discharging processes have been placed in GCD curves, which confirms these results very well. Also, GCD curves with symmetrical shape exhibit the extremely reversible charge/discharge behavior, through the redox reaction which endorses the great electrochemical efficiency. The specific capacitance values against the current density that obtained from the GCD plots show in Fig. 8c. Obviously, the discharge time has an inverse correlation with the current density, so the discharge time of 2 Ag−1, because of the enough time to diffuse into the active electrode material of is the best and shows the highest capacitance value. The specific capacitance which calculated from the GCD plots were evaluated to be 1859.2, 1224.9, 981.8, and 286.2 Fg−1 (878, 587.1, 466, and 137.1 Cg−1) at 2, 3, 4, and 5 Ag−1 respectively. Various current densities from 2 to 5 Ag−1 were used at various potentials between 0.1 V and 0.57 V in order to estimate and better understand the specific capacitance of the NHV/NF, NHV-NGC1/NF and NHV-NGC3/NF electrodes.

Fig. 9(a,c, and e) illustrates that electrodes store charge as pseudo-capacitance, and faradaic redox reactions are demonstrated throughout the charging and discharging processes, which confirms these results. Additionally, GCD curves with symmetrical shapes exhibit extremely reversible charge/discharge behavior, due to the redox reaction, which supports a high electrochemical efficiency. According to the GCD plots, the specific capacitance values against the current density are shown in Fig. 9(b, d, and e). The discharge time has an inverse relationship with the current density, so the discharge time of 2 Ag−1, because the active electrode material has had enough time to diffuse into the electrode, shows the highest capacitance value.

A supercapacitor's real application in numerous systems is determined by its energy density and power density. Using Eqs. 3 and 4, power density and energy density were estimated from GCD curves at different current densities (Table 2). It can be seen that the maximum energy density was about 57.8 Whkg−1 (with power density 473.9 WKg−1) at 2 Ag−1, and the maximum power density was about 1196.7 Wkg−1 (with power density 9.1 Whkg-1) at 5 Ag−1. The comparison between electrical conductivity of the NHV-NGC2/NF and NHV/NF nanostructures were studied by EIS analysis. Fig. 10a, illustrated the EIS spectra of these electrodes at open circuit potential (OCP) in 3.0 M KOH. As can be seen, the EIS diagram of NHV-NGC2/NF electrode has a steeper slope than NHV/NF electrode, which illustrate better capacitive properties. The value of charge transfer resistance (Rct) of NHV-NGC2/NF is lower than NHV/NF which indicated improving electrical conductivity by adding the optimal amount of N-GQD/cellulose to the final nanocomposite. The values of EIS fitting parameters are summarized in the table Fig. 10b. The fitting curves illustrate in Fig. 10c, d for pristine NHV and NHV-NGC2 composite, respectively.

For investigation of the practical application, the cycle stability of NHV-NGC2/NF at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1 for 3000 of CV was carried. According to Fig. 11, after 100 cycles of charge-discharge, the retention capacitance value increased due to the activation process. With increasing time, the retention capacitance value gradually decreased. In addition, the retention capacitance of NHV-NGC2/NF after 3000 cycles is 83.5% of the initial capacitance, which demonstrates the long-term cycle stability of NHV-NGC2/NF as supercapacitor electrodes. Based on these results, we were able to demonstrate synergistic effects between each component of the nanocomposite and the electrochemical stability created by N-GQDs-cellulose in the NHV-NGC2 nanocomposites.

Nitrogen serves two essential purposes in the production of N-GQDs: (i) Pyridinic nitrogen oxide was capable of producing electron-rich carbon atoms (adjacent carbon), which made it easier to adsorb hydrogen. As more hydrogen leaves the effective site as H2 in an effort to bond with another H+ , the effective site grows. (ii) The presence of nitrogen boosts the conductivity of a material by creating structural networks [54]. HNV-NGC has a high efficiency because of the trap states created by both dopants and edge states, which may absorb charge carriers. By doping with electron acceptors or electron donors, it is possible to change the electron density of graphene layers. In graphene, carbon may take the place of nitrogen as heteroatoms. As a result of electron migration, electrons and holes are produced. In electrochemical and electrocatalyst systems with energy storage applications, N-GQDs collaborate with each other to change the structure [55]. Due to its ability to electrically change graphene structures, nitrogen can enhance their conductivity and catalytic properties.

According to the results, NHV-NGC electrodes have a high specific capacitance, attributed to the layered structure of nanocomposites formed from nanoribbons and quantum dots in cellulose to form 3D flower-shaped microstructures. Furthermore, the synergistic effects between each component facilitate the ion/electron transition, thereby improving the specific capacitance. In order for flexible electronics to be developed sustainably, it is necessary to use cellulose-based nanocomposites as a variety of functional building blocks for supercapacitors. This three-electrode manufactured supercapacitor has a very high energy density of 57.8 Whkg−1 at a power density of 473.9 Wkg−1. Based on the findings of the study, the NHV-NGC electrode, as prepared, had a specific capacitance of 1852 Fg−1 (878 Cg−1), at a current density of 2 Ag−1, when used in the aqueous electrolyte of 3.0 M KOH. After modification with NGC components, the specific capacitance of NHV electrodes increased to approximately 631 Fg−1 (300Cg−1) from about 631 Fg−1 (300Cg−1). After 3000 charge-discharge cycles at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1, the electrode exhibits better retention specific capacitance of 83.5%. For the optimized NGC electrode, our charge transfer resistance (Rct) value is 4.50 in comparison to 8.28 in the pristine electrode. This indicates that by adding the appropriate amount of N-GQD/cellulose, the electrode exhibits better electrical conductivity.
